title: Layered colour blending in wet felting
subtitle: Controlling how colours merge, separate, or streak by varying fibre orientation and layer order
excerpt: The way colours appear in a finished wet-felted panel depends entirely on how the fibres are laid before felting. Cross-hatched layers of different colours produce a heathered blend; same-direction layers with thin overlap produce distinct bands with a gradient edge; thick wisps of a third colour added on top produce streaks. This technique entry covers the three main approaches and how to predict the result.
sourceNotes: Colour-blending in wet felting is observation-led technique. The three methods described reflect the standard approaches taught in textile-art workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A colour effect produced by cross-hatching two colours in alternating layers before felting. The fibres intermingle during the felting process and produce a flecked or tweedy mix rather than distinct colour bands.
A line of contrasting colour running through a wet-felted panel, created by placing a thin rope of roving on top of the laid layers in the direction of felting before wetting out.

content.text: Colour in wet felt is set by the lay, not by mixing as in paint or dye. A
comes from cross-hatching. A distinct gradient comes from thinning the overlap zone. A
comes from a surface rope of roving left unblended on top of the lay. Each method is laid on a standard two-to-three-layer base of the main colour and felted the same way as a plain panel.

items.name: Merino roving, colour B (blend or streak)
Hot water and cold water access

content.text: Lay one layer of colour A horizontally. Lay one layer of colour B vertically. Lay a second layer of colour A vertically, and a second of colour B horizontally. The four layers alternate in direction and colour; when felted, the fibres intermingle and produce a tweedy, even mix. The closer the colours, the smoother the blend; complementary colours produce a lively textured surface.
Method 2: Distinct bands with soft edges

Lay two layers of colour A across the full width. Lay two layers of colour B starting from one end, reaching two-thirds of the way. Where the two colours meet, tease both out to wisps and overlap the wisps by 3-4 cm; this is the gradient zone. Wet out and felt as normal. The gradient zone will felt to a soft blended band; the sections beyond it will read as distinct colours.

Lay a standard two-layer base of colour A. Before wetting, place thin ropes of colour B on top of the base, running in the direction you want the streaks to travel (usually parallel to the rolling direction). Ropes should be thin, perhaps 5 mm across. Wet out very gently through the net without disturbing the ropes. Rub lightly until the surface locks. The surface ropes felt in place but retain a raised linear texture distinctly different from the blended base.

items.problem: The heathered blend comes out as a solid muddy colour rather than a mix
items.cause: Colours too similar in value (both light or both dark), or layers rubbed too vigorously before the fibres lock
items.fix: Use colours with a value contrast of at least two stops. Rub gently in the early stage; vigorous rubbing before the fibres lock blends them into a solid.

items.problem: The streak ropes disappear into the background
items.cause: Ropes too thin, or rubbed over too early during wetting
items.fix: Make the rope ropes slightly thicker, and place the net over them without rubbing before the surface has begun to lock.

items.problem: The gradient zone is too abrupt and reads as a line rather than a blend
items.cause: The overlap zone was too short or the wisps were not thin enough
items.fix: Extend the overlap zone to at least 5 cm and pull wisps to translucency before laying.
